I am so excited for the session and for everything. I've had so much fun and we just like two or three hours into the thing. So I can't even wait for the next few days. All right. So a couple of things before we get started. First off, as I've been looking at our community as a whole, as ClickFunnels, FunnelHackers, I feel like we're getting really, really, really good at building funnels and sales structures and all sorts of things like that. As I look at a lot of the funnels, I see what I feel like is lacking is the ability to actually sell and to do it in a cool way, to help serve people and inspires them and makes them want to follow you into your mass movement. So what I wanted to talk about for the next hour or so is understanding the right way to sell and how to do in a really cool, fun, exciting way. Before we do, I'm excited for this session for a couple of reasons. One of them is about five and a half, six years ago, I was in a funk in my business and my life where we had grown a huge company, we had about a hundred employees, the whole thing collapsed around me and I was just like done and tired. And I was inspired to create and to serve and do all the stuff that we do as entrepreneurs. And I kind of just wanted to give up. I remember if you've read the Dotcom Seekers book, I talked about this waking up in the morning and like wishing that I had a boss so they would fire me so I could just go back to bed, which is a horrible feeling, right? We shouldn't be feeling that way if we're here to inspire. And I had a close friend of mine who reached out to me during this dark period in my life and reignited the spark in this business. And what's interesting is that what got me excited again was this topic and this presentation. And it started with a couple of phone calls and he started talking to me about ideas that were just, I hadn't thought about in a long time. It was how to create belief in your customers and how to get them excited and motivated and how to do it through stories and all sorts of stuff. And him getting me refired up took me on a journey for the last four or five years. It's brought me to where I'm today with all of you amazing people here. And I just wanted, I last week I called him and I was like, man, I want you here at this event for a couple of reasons. First off, if it wasn't for him reaching out to me during a dark time, none of us, I don't think would be here. And he's someone who's just super inspiring to me and that's like honestly changed my life. And I think he's back here. I just want to give him some credit before we get started. Dagen, are you here? This is Dagen Smith. Everyone, let's give him a huge round of applause. One of the coolest people ever and one of the best marketing minds our industry's ever had and just so grateful for him. Now, when we started this conversation, he started a message in me. He was talking initially about movies and books and all these things and about how authors create belief and desire and all these things. And it like started to spark in me. I started geeking out and getting excited and it took me on this huge journey for four or five years to what is I'm going to present today. And it's stuff that I've talked about in little pieces in different spots. And he has pieces of this on my podcast or different events, things like that. This first time I had a chance to kind of lay it out in one whole thing. And there's so much of the stuff came initially from thoughts and conversations Dagen and I had. And I just want to kind of dedicate this to him. The new expert secrets book you guys will see is actually dedicated him because it was the foundation conversation. Him is the foundation where this book came from and just grateful for him and and excited. All right. So the topic that we're going to be talking about right now is how to create belief. It's interesting. Like I used to think that our job as marketers was to sell people stuff. Right. So I learned all these sales skills. I read all the sales books and like how to close and all these kind of things. And it got me to a spot, but I couldn't grow beyond that. Like it was just it was stagnant and it wasn't inspiring. It didn't cause like the the change in people's lives that I wanted to have. Right. Selling is good because you make money. That's nice. Right. But what's better is when you're doing it in a way that like causes beliefs to change in people, which causes them to move and chosen to change their lives as well. And that's like the fascinating, like the coolest part about this. So this whole session is about how to create belief. Okay. If you're to water down or take everything that marketing is the whole and like, what is the goal and the purpose of it? It's this is to create belief in the minds and the hearts of your customers. And if you do that, if they honestly believe that what you are saying is true, they're going to follow you. That's it. So how do we create that belief and how do we do it in a way that that's really cool and fun? So a couple of core things. I want to kind of conceptual things I want to talk about first. And we're going to dig deeper into story. The first thing is this concept of the one thing. So I was at an event four or five years ago and listening to a bunch of great marketers are sitting in the audience and Tim Ferriss was on stage. How many of you guys have ever seen Tim Ferriss before or heard him read his books? Okay. He's awesome. Right? And he's on stage and somebody came up during the Q and a session and asked them, it's like, so Tim Ferriss, what's your day look like? And he kind of laughed. He said, you know what? I get asked that all the time and people have asked if they come film day in the life of Tim Ferriss. He's like, I always tell him, no, and you want to know why? He said, most people wake up in the morning. They got us to do this of a thousand things and they're doing thing after thing after thing after all these kinds of things trying to get done and they're busy and they're moving all the time. He's like, if you look at my life, it's not that way at all. He's like, I wake up in the morning. I drink some coffee or some tea. I sit there and I think and then I read that I go on a walk and I'm thinking and I'll do that for three or four or five weeks before I ever do anything. He said, what I'm doing those, I'm trying to spend that time thinking about not all the millions of dominoes I got to knock down. I'm trying to identify what's the one huge domino. If I can knock that thing down, we'll knock down every other domino that's in the way or make the rest of them irrelevant. We said that I had this like, aha, in my head. I was like, that's the key. Then a little while later, I was talking to my friend, Perry Belcher, one of the owners at Digital Marketer. Perry was saying, he said, the interesting thing at that, he said, we went through all the offers we've created in the last 10 years. He said, anytime we ask somebody to believe more than one thing in our sales message, it was always a bomb. He said, if the message, the core message got them to believe just one thing, those were our winners every single time. Anytime we made it more complex, starting to believe one or two or three or four things, the message just didn't work. So I started thinking about that. I started realizing that if I want somebody to buy something from me, I got to get them to believe something. What's the one thing they have to believe? They believe this one thing, then they have to give me money. What is that thing? So for all of you guys, it's going to be different. We did an event for our inner circle members probably eight or nine months ago, and I was talking about this, and I was planning on spending about 15 minutes on it. And as we started going deeper and deeper, we ended up spending about three hours trying to figure out this thing. And from that three hour session popped out one sentence that was the key. Do you guys want to see the sentence? All right, this is what it is. This is the big domino statement. If I can make them believe that my new opportunity, we talked about this morning, is the key to whatever they desire most, and it's only attainable through my specific vehicle, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant, and they have to give me money. That's what we have to create for anything you're selling. If you can create that, that's the key. Now this seems really, really easy until you start doing it, and it's not. It takes a while. So I'm going to show you guys a couple of examples. So for my business, this is what my domino statement would look like. If I can get them to believe that funnels, this is my vehicle, is the key to their online business success, and it's only attainable through click funnels, if they honestly, for me to be successful, I have to have a funnel, and the only way to have a funnel is click funnels, if you honestly believe that you have to give me money, there's no other, if you don't give me money, you're lying to yourself. That's the argument I want to have. If I can create something so true that if they believe that, they have to give me money or else they're lying to themselves. That's the key. If they do that, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. So if you guys want to start thinking through your business, can you create that statement for what it is you're selling? Now as we started doing this in the inner circle, I started getting a bunch of them back from people and they got the gist, like, cool, I got it, here it is, what's the next thing? And everyone's came in kind of like this. If I can get them to believe that cutting calories and exercising is the key to losing weight and it's only attainable through my new weight loss course, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. Was that a true statement? No, it's not true. Okay, if I convince someone you have to cut calories and exercise to lose weight, do you have to give me money? No, you can give anybody money. Everyone teaches this. There's a billion other people. You haven't done anything, right? All you've done is make them believe that the thing as a whole is true and not what you have is the only path to success. I have to believe that this is the only path to success and if I can get them to believe that, then they're part of it, then they're going to join, right? So this statement is not true for a bunch of reasons. Number one, it's not true because you are not in the prolific zone. Everyone's teaching how to cut calories. I'm right here in the middle, not prolific. I can go to school and learn that. I can go on Google and learn that. I can get everything, it's free. Everyone's got that, right? So it's not prolific. Number two, this is not a new opportunity. Okay, I'm not presenting any kind of new opportunity. Number three, this is not an ablution. Okay, so if you create a statement as you start doing this, I wish we had an hour we could just spend just workshopping and just doing this. But if what you have doesn't fit, if it's not a true statement, then these are the reasons why. So if I was in the weight loss space, I'm going to transform this a little bit. So this is how I would do something. And I'm going to tell a story about this a little while later, but there's a network marketing company called Proovit and I helped them build out some of their initial messaging and sales funnels and we got some approvers here in the audience. And so for that group, when we were kind of working through how I was going to write the sales pitch and the messaging for them, this is what I came up with. Okay, if I can make them believe that getting their body into state of ketosis, that's the vehicle, is the key to losing weight and it's only attainable through drinking Proovit's ketones, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money. Do you see how much stronger that is? If I can convince you that you have to be in ketosis, lose weight. That's the only vehicle that's going to work for you. And the only way to do that is by drinking this magic drink. That's it. You have to give me money or else you're lying to yourself. Or you're not really desiring the end result. Okay, so that is the key. So I want all you guys think for a second for yourself, what would this sentence be for you? What would the big domino statement be for you? Like this is what you have to create before we can begin to believe something because this is what we have to begin to believe. Okay, so tonight this is your homework assignment. I want all of you guys to create a domino statement. And at first you can think it's to take 10 seconds and an hour into your day. Gosh, this is harder than I thought. Turns out I'm selling an improvement offer. Huh. Turns out I am in a red ocean with everybody else. Huh. This statement makes you makes all this talk about earlier have to happen. It can't not happen unless you if you fit in the statement. Okay. All right. So now after you have this big domino, then my next question, why you think through is why do you personally believe that's true? Okay. What was the experience that you had that made that true for you? Okay. I want to tell a story that'll kind of illustrate this. So after I did the sales presentation for, for prove it, this is the company. This is their drink. So it's a drink. You drink it, put your body in a state of ketosis. Very 10% of you guys know what that means. Everyone else is like, what is he talking about? Right. So after, after I helped them write initial, initial video that they've used to help grow the company and, and it was kind of cool. And afterwards, Brian, who's the only company, he said, Hey, we're doing a big event out in, I think it was in Vegas. And he's like, can you come and speak to the leadership team about like how they can use this to help grow the company? And I was like, okay, that'd be kind of fun. So I fly out there to, to Vegas and I get there and I land and I'm walking to the hotel and what I see, and I love network markers, I'm going to teach you guys all for a little bit for those who are in that profession. I walk in the hallways. I'm walking to go find Brian and I'm walking down the hall. I see all these people and all of them wearing T-shirts to say glucose is our ketones are greater than glucose. Okay. And I'm like, I don't even know what that means. And I wrote the pitch for this thing. Okay. And they're all walking and they're rocking around and like, they're pouncing on people and like, Hey, you need to get your body in ketosis. And like, you need to drink this. And like, they're pouncing all these people. And I was like, huh. So I get there to the, and walking through this thing and I'm watching this process happen, you know, and I get to the hotel room and I find Brian and I was like, Hey, man, how's it going? We were talking. And I said, um, so what do you want me to talk about tonight? At the, at the, at the thing, he's like, Oh, whatever you want to talk about, man. It's cool. I was like, okay, well, I don't know. Okay. Give me some direction. Like, do you have anything in, in, in mind? He's like, no, whatever you think will be good. I'm like, okay, what time are we on? He's like, Oh crap. It started, it started like 10 minutes ago. I'm like, like right now he's like, yeah, we got to go. So he jumps up and we start and we're actually filming this. So I have footage. You guys will see it when you see Funnel Hacker TV. We film this whole thing. And I'm, so he's running the elevator. I'm like, what do you want me to talk about? I said, whatever you want, man, don't worry about it. We come in this room and there's a room of a hundred of the top leaders in the company and they're all sitting there, like going crazy, excited. And Brian walks in and they walk to the front and grabs the microphone. It introduces me and then sets it down. I'm in the back of my backpack and flip flops on. And I'm like, what to do right now? So I kind of walk to the front and I get up there and I turn around and look out in this audience and I look in their eyes and it hit me what they needed to hear. And I looked out there and I said, okay, I've been watching you guys for the last hours. I'm walking through the lobby. And what's interesting is that for everyone, the people in this group, and I'm going to tell the story about them and I'm kind of picking on them a little bit. But this is true for all of you guys. So to take this personal, this is the point of it. So I said that what's happening is all you guys are going and you're, and you're, you're, you're doing this, you're doing this wrong. And I said, if you look at yourself, this was you, I don't know, six months ago, a year ago, and you were just a normal dude, happiest can be going on your normal life, doing whatever you were doing. And all of a sudden somebody came to you and they, and something happened, right? I don't know if somebody gave you this stuff, you drank it, you liked it or some process happened, something happened. They gave you this epiphany where you're like, huh? I should be drinking this stuff. Right. And for you, for you, I want you to plug in whatever your business is, right? You guys are weight loss, finance, like whatever, whatever it was for you, something happened, some experience happened in your life. And all of a sudden you're like, huh? And you had this epiphany, right? And it's epiphany happen. And after you had this epiphany, you got excited. Like, this is so cool. I remember like when I had my epiphany out funnels, I'm like, this is the coolest thing in the world. I started geeking out, right? And so I go on this huge journey over here. I go on this journey. I'm over here and I'm studying and I'm learning and I'm doing the worst thing that marketers could ever do is we start geeking out on the topic, right? So I'll put glasses on this person because they're like totally geeked out, right? And you start studying all the math behind it and the science and the reasons why. And now this is better than that. And that all you get, all this stuff, you get everything, right? And you start storing it away in this cloud. And I have a friend, her name is Kim Claver. She wrote a book called, If Your Product's So Great, How Come Nobody Overbuy It? And one of the chapters is called Techno Babble. And she says, this is the reason why most people can't sell. They learn all this stuff, all these things. They're studying the back and they're like, you know, all the, all the ingredients and the reason, the science and they're so excited by this thing, right? And then they go and they're in a hotel and they see like their prospect walking bass and like, oh my gosh, this is it. This is my chance. Like I have a chance to sell this person. We're going to change their whole life and the person comes out and then they pounce on the person, right? They pounce on them. They start doing all, they start spewing out all this techno babble. And they're like, look, you need to be drinking this stuff because I don't know if you know, but ketones are greater than glucose. And if you had beta hydroxybutyrate salts in your body, you'd be melting fat off and they start doing all this stuff. And the poor prospect is like getting this techno babble, just shot at him and they're like, go like, I don't know what to do. Right. And then they run away and they're like, oh, nobody will buy my stuff. Right. You know, this is, this is how most of us are selling. Okay. Most of you guys, this is the promise that you're getting stuck in the techno babble. Okay. Now the techno babble is not what got you to buy. It's not what's going to get them to buy. You were back here and something happened. You had an emotional experience. They got you excited. Then you went on a journey over here and then logically, can you guys see this over here? Oh, we have a camera. Good. Um, and then logically you convince yourself, this is the coolest thing in the world, but then you meet this prospect and you try to jam logic down their throat to sell them. And that's not how you sell but anything, anybody, anything. Okay. The way you sell stuff is to stop and forget who you are right now. And you've got to come backwards on this journey and come back here and remember, what was it that gave you this epiphany? Cause that's the key. If you can remember that piece, that's how you sell somebody. Okay. It's not by giving them all this techno babble. This is what kills all your cells. It's the epiphany, the story that you tell it gets somebody to believe. Okay. So I want you to think back for your product. What was it? Why did you believe that domino statement? What was the experience you had? You didn't wake up one day and you wanted to be teaching fitness or finance or gardening or whatever your thing is. You just wake up when I want to do this. Like something happened and we have to remember that experience. So I wrote, when I wrote the pitch for this company, um, I, I'm not a health guru. As you can probably tell, but I like it. Right. And, and, um, Brian had actually written a script for this company ahead of time. And he sent it to me. He was like, Hey man, here's a script. Can you, can you look at it and give me your feedback? And I was reading it. I was like, huh, I'm like, it's, it's, it's not very good. He had all this techno babble. He was dropping all these like fancy words and, uh, Brian's been a friend for a long time. So I'm sitting in bed when I reading a script, trying to edit it for him. Like, you know what, this is not even any good. So I just highlighted the whole thing and deleted it as I'm just going to write this thing from scratch. And so as I sat there, I'm like, well, I've heard of this thing called ketosis. Like, what was it? Like what gave me the epiphany that this was a cool thing to do? I started thinking back and I remembered about a year and a half earlier. I was at, uh, uh, I was at a seminar and then I went to a dinner with one of my friends's names, Aaron Lilly. And we got to eat and I'm ordering like all sorts of good food and Aaron ordered a chicken with a side of butter. And I said, what are you doing? He's like, Oh, it's really good. You dip your chicken in butter and then you eat it. I was like, aren't you going to die? Like, isn't that really bad for you? I don't know. My mom always didn't let me have butter. I don't know. And, uh, and he was like, no, this is really, really good. And I was like, well, explain to me. It doesn't make sense to me. He said, okay. He's like my body and he's like ripped and looked awesome. And I was like, I'd like to look like you. Maybe I should just get some butter. And, um, he started explaining ketosis. He said, he said, look at, he said, if you think about carbs, carbs are like kinlings, they take a bunch of kindling, you throw it on a fire. What happens? I was like, flames go up and then they go back down. He's like, yeah, so you get energy for like 20 seconds and then you're like, you're shot, right? He's like, that's happened to me at Cheerios for breakfast. You're like, uh, and then it's gone. Right. Um, sitting in the frosted flakes, like you're trying to be Tony the tiger and the 10 seconds later it's gone. Right. He's like, no, if you look at protein, protein is like a log. You throw that on fire and it burns a little bit longer, but eventually it's still going to burn out. He's like, if you get your body's burning fat, it's like putting coal on the fire. And the coal is like, it'll, it'll, uh, if you catch that on fire, it'll stay, stay warm all the time. He's like, I've trained my body to burn fats. And so because of that, like, um, uh, like I can eat this stuff all day long. And I never gained weight and I feel awesome and it was amazing. And so he told me that and I remember that, like that was what gave me the epiphany. So when I sat down that night at two in the morning to write the script for prove it, all I did is I told the story. If anyone who's ever seen that video, they call inside the company, the campfire video and I have a little cartoon video of me talking about how the difference, uh, how you have a campfire and it's like throwing, uh, carbs, like throwing, uh, kindling on the fire and protein and coal. I just told that story in a video. They took that video, um, they went out and within the first six months of using that video, all the company puts on their phones and they show people and then they sign them up. Like it's all they do. Uh, the first six months the company did $20 million in sales. And this, I think this year they're on track to like $250 million or more from that one epiphany bridge story. Okay. Is that cool? And a whole bunch of amazing people who are out sparing the message, but that was the key. It's remembering. If I come back and spew all the techno babble, nobody's going to buy your thing. And this is where most of you guys are selling from. So the key is to stop and step back and remember what was the epiphany that you had? Okay. And so that's the next slide here is what was the origin story? What was the thing that made you believe that thing? Okay. All right. Now to kind of go a little bit deeper with this, um, the next problem that most of you guys are going to have is you're going to start telling that story, but then the problem is you're still going to slip back into this techno babble. Right. For some reason we love these words, but they feel so smart. Our status goes up every time we say them and we're like, Oh, I know these big words. How many of you guys know what beta hydroxy butyrate salts are? Cause I do that guy knows. That's how cool I am. Right. And so we always want to spew the stuff out. Right. And so I found anytime you do a sales message, you have to stop yourself from using techno babble. And sometimes you feel like I have to use it because like it's a big word and how do I make it simple? So I learned a little trick and I call these kind of like bridges. Okay. And what it kind of like bridges is every time I'm doing, I'm giving a message and I hit a word that's kind of like techno babble. I have to stop because if I don't stop, if I say techno babble war and I keep moving, everyone's like, what do you just say? And they get stuck and they're like, uh, and then the whole sales gone. Right. So every time I hit a word that's a big confusing word or using any kind of techno babble, I have to stop and I say, you know, it's kind of like, and I liken it to something they understand. So I wrote the pitch for prove it. Um, ketones is the word that most people don't know. So as soon as I said ketones, I'm like, da, da, da, da, da ketones. I'm like, it's kind of like a million motivational speakers running through your body and like, Oh, cool. I get that. Right. Okay. Uh, I talk about how it gives you energy. I'm like, yeah, it gives you really, really cool energy. It's kind of like, if you guys have played that game Pac-Man, you know, in Pac-Man, the whole time is like running away from the ghosts, but every once while he eats those power pellets and then like he gets on and then he's, I start chasing the ghosts and eating them. Like that's what it feels like. It's kind of like that. Okay. So kind of like, I use these, how many of you guys have heard me say at least 3000 kind of likes in the last 12 hours, six hours, right? Every time I say any word that I'm like, somebody might not understand that I stopped, I'm like, Oh, it's kind of like this. And then they're like, Oh, cool. Okay. So this is the key. Like whenever you're telling a story, the kind of like bridge is the thing is going to save you from techno babble. Cause most of you guys are going to want to keep slipping back there and getting back there over and over again. And it's the key to breaking that. Okay. All right. Um, okay. So the next level of 50 bridges. So this is something that, uh, I learned from Dagan and it's the coolest thing is part of, part of telling the story is telling the story. But if you ever noticed that sometimes, um, you'll try to tell someone a story and you're like, okay, this is the deal. And you start telling them what happened and you're so excited and you explain it to them. And then they're like, Oh, wow, that sounds cool. You're like, no, no, no, no, like, dude, and you retell the whole story again. And like, no, I heard you. I, it's cool, man. You're like, no, like you had to be there. If you'd have been there, you would have got it. Right. Who's ever said that before? Okay. It's frustrating. Right. And you think about it. The reason why is because, um, because like the person wasn't, didn't have the same experience you do. And so what you have to understand, I learned, I learned this piece of it from, um, from Tony Robbins. When I was at his event, he talked a lot about state and state control. And he may talk about that in two days from now when Tony's here, which is exciting. Um, but if I want somebody to have the same epiphany that I had, I can't just tell it to him. I can't just like give it to him. Like here's the epiphany I had and shove it down their throat. Cause they're like, they're not going to get it. What I have to do is I have to get them in the same state that I was in. Right. So here's the state that I was in when I had this epiphany. I have to get them into that same state. And if I do that, then they'll have the same epiphany that I had. Okay. So let me kind of explain that a little deeper. Um, uh, actually I'm going to illustrate this to, I think it'll make it, you make more sense. Um, so if you think about good books, like the really, like your best authors that, right, amazing books, right? They won't just like tell a story that like they walk into a scene and like, Hey, this is what happened. And then it kind of ends, right? Like they'll spend 10 or 20 or 30 pages, like setting up the scene and how they felt and the, the, the lights and the sounds and everything. So that you get in the same state as the character. So when you're like, then the character has this experience, you're like, Whoa, I felt that. Right. And so good authors will do a good job of that. Film is amazing. Cause in film, they can do so much more than we can do typically when we're just speaking like this, right? Um, and this is a film, uh, about the time that I was going through this, this deep dark spot in my life. Uh, and Dagen, I were talking about this and he kept saying, he's like, the key to stories, you have to get them to feel. And I remember he said, he's like, have you ever seen the movie, uh, the new X-Men movie that's out at the time? He's like, it's the one where they were all little kids. I was like, yeah, he's like, do you remember that scene? And so there was a scene where a magneto, he has no magneto who becomes the bad guy in the future movies. He's a little kid and they're taking him and his family into this Nazi concentration camp. And as they're pulling him in, they see the fences, the metal fences kind of move. And the Nazis are like, okay, there's something wrong with this kid. And so they bring him into a, to a private room, right? And in that room, um, they're trying to get him, they want to see what his powers are and so they can understand them so they can use them. Right. And so, uh, in this scene, they, uh, they have him and they're trying to get him to move a, move a coin on a desk and trying to move. And, um, and then they also bring in his mom and they're telling him, like, you need to move this coin. And so he's trying to move the coin, trying to move the coin, and he can't quite move it. And so finally the Nazi guy stands up, shoots his mom, his mom falls dead. And then all of a sudden you see for a second where he looks away at his mom, and he comes back with like this look of fear and, and like just scared. He was just scared. And all of a sudden that fear turns into like anger and rage. And you watch the scene where he just destroys the, the entire room. I'm going to show you guys the scene really quick. Cause I want you, as you're watching this to notice how you, how you feel. Okay. Good film, good movies, give us, they'd have it, uh, they do such a good job beginning us to feel. And I want you guys to see this and I can explain how we can, how we can model that in our storytelling. So let me show you guys this clip real quick. All right. See, it's good. All right. All right. Try. No. Cool. acceptable. Yup. Come on. Your Avelour daddeliver. No! Mausous! NOOOOO! He's able to feel that? Isn't that amazing? Now if Magneto was like a lot of us telling stories 20 years later, he'd be like, yeah, so when I was a kid I was in a Nazi concentration camp and they wanted me to move a coin and I couldn't so they shot my mom and that sucked. That's how a lot of you guys tell your stories. I've heard it. And I get rust, why is my funnel converting? Seriously? Like, I don't know. If we want them to have the same epiphany we had, we have to get them into the same state that we were in when we had that epiphany. We do that by getting people to feel what we felt. So when I'm telling a story instead of just saying, hey, when I got started in this business, I wanted to make some money and so I started making these potato gun things and I made funnels and I made a bunch of money, it was really cool. Nobody feels that. Nobody's going to experience what you experience. I'm going to come back and remember the pain and remember how I felt and then I explain it. So I'm explaining something like this. I'll just make a story at the top of my head. I would say something like, it was my senior year in college. I was sitting there in an auditorium without a thousand of my peers. We were all about to graduate. And I remember sitting there looking around and realizing that I hadn't learned anything in school. Like, I had nothing that I was going to go out there. I'm looking around, everyone's celebrating their partying and they're having all this fun. I was like, do you guys not realize? I remember feeling this like pit in my stomach and it almost felt like a heart attack, but it was deeper. It was like down here and I felt like it was stabbing. And I was like, how in the world are we supposed to go and actually go out there in the real world and like, I have no skills that are actually marketable. And I remember feeling this weight on my back. So I just got married early that year. I was like, how am I supposed to support my wife? And if we want kids in the future and this weight kept getting heavier and heavier and the more that I thought about it, the more I was just like, I don't know how I'm going to do this. How many of you guys, as I was explaining that story, felt pressure on your shoulder. Felt pain. You felt some of those things, right? That's actually a true story. It was really painful. I think heavens were funnels. But if you tell a story that way and you explain how you felt and the intro, all the stuff that's happening, the dialogue in your head and you get just like an author when they're writing a book and you get people in that state. When you come and you tell this story, you're not telling them out. Here's the epiphany I had. You tell them that story and then they have the epiphany. Last year at this event, before Marcus Lamona spoke, we had a 30 minute window where I had a chance to meet and we wrote that in the contract. I'm my trick to hang out with cool people. I pay a bunch of money, you get a 30 minute contract before they speak on stage. So we're sitting there in this meeting, we're talking and stuff and he's like, and he kind of walked by the window. He came to say, so what do you guys do again? We're like, oh, we have this thing called click funnels and it builds websites. He's like, why is everyone in the room so excited then? And I'm like, what do you mean? He's like, you have a website build that people are jumping and screaming. Everyone's like, I don't understand what you guys are doing. And I'm like, okay. And so all of a sudden I instantly slipped into techno babble. I tried to explain a sales funnel and the upsells and all this stuff. And he was like, huh, I don't get it. Why are they so excited? And I stopped and I was like, okay, well, and I tried to really quickly remember, like, well, what was, why am I so excited by this? Why is everyone in the room excited? And so I kind of explained a story about how funnels, what they are and how they work. And he's listening. He said, okay. He's like, well, how will this work for camping world? So I'm like, well, I'd probably do this. He's like, well, how would this work for sweet pizza? How would this work? And he asked me three or four companies and he stopped and he's like, huh, every company needs a funnel. I was like, yeah, let's say do. But I didn't tell him, hey, Marcus, every company needs a funnel. He'd be like, okay, whatever. And he had the epiphany. He said, every company needs a funnel. Do you want to be on my show? Yes, I do. And six months later on the show, but it's because I didn't force this epiphany on his thing. I explained it through story and he had the epiphany. If you guys learn nothing else from this weekend, this is probably the most valuable thing I can give you. When you understand this, this is how you sell. It's not about sweet headlines and like copyrighting tricks and secrets, all sorts of stuff. It's just remembering what got you to believe what you do. There's a reason why you're so passionate about what you do. There's a reason why you flew around the world to be here, to hang out with all of us, talking about funnels and all these things. Cause you, there's something that you experienced and you have to remember what that was. And that's the story you got to tell. Okay. It's not my story. It's your story. If you remember what that was and why you cared about it and you get really good at sharing that, that's what gets people to come over here. That's what gets people to follow your mass movement. That's what gets people to believe in you because you believe in it. Hey, do any of you guys have any doubt that I believe my message a hundred percent? Okay. I told the story over and over and over again, cause I know that if you guys believe that I believe you're going to come, but you got to be, it's all part of this. Okay. All right. So that's, that's the epiphany bridge right here. Now, after we kind of start figuring these things out and really understanding it, simplifying the kind of like bridges and how to get people in state. The next question is like, well, is there structure? Like how can I replicate this over and over again? So some stories aren't amazing and some stories are big failures. Like how do I replicate this? And about five years ago, same time, Dagen and I started hanging out talking again, we would decide to do this workshop. And at the time he'd gotten me onto this course by two guys, Michael Hague and Chris Vogler, and it was called the heroes two journeys. And he's like, dude, you got to listen to this. I was listening to it. He was, and we're going back and forth. Like this stuff is amazing. And these guys are Hollywood screenwriting screenwriters and our consultants. And so they go in and in this course, they were talking about like, if you look at every holiday Hollywood movie from the beginning of time till now, almost every successful movie follows the exact same pattern. You said, you can look at like the screenplay of Titanic and the screenplay from any other movie you think of like Star Wars, right? And if you flip through them, you'll see like on the same page, both of them is when this happens to the hero on the same page is what this happens and this happens. And like the whole thing is, is the same story lines. And so Dagen, I asked if we can get Michael Hague to come speak and he came and he spoke for like six hours of our event, all about story and the heroes two journeys. And it was like the coolest thing in the world, but I had no idea like how, how to get it to work for like what I do. Cause it was really so big and complex. And I spent the next four and a half, five years trying to figure this out. And, uh, and I feel like in the last six to eight months or so, like, I've really kind of figured it out. And so I want to do it now for the next, I got 25 minutes left. I want to, I want to go through the heroes two journeys. Um, first off, you understand the framework of how good stories are told. And then from there, I'm going to show you as a compressed, not a compressed version, but a simple way to tell the heroes two journeys for your Pifny bridge. And when you understand the, the hierarchy of how the heroes two journey works along with what we're going to give you guys at the end of this, it's going to make your storytelling process so simple and so easy. All right. So this right here, after listening to Michael Hague, uh, and going through his books and his courses, I like, for me, I'm very visual. I sketched out. I'm like, okay, this is it. And it took me a long time to figure this whole thing. I sketched it all. And that is the map of the heroes two journeys. If you go through all his stuff, it all boils down to that picture right there. Like that's everything you need to understand. So I'm going to kind of walk you guys piece by piece. So you understand it. Okay. And one thing Michael said, he said that all stories are simple at their core. Okay. So you can make it more complex and then go from, you know, a 30 second story to like a four or five hour movie, but it is, it is essence that are very simple. He said that every story's got a couple of things. First off, there are three core things to every story. There's a character that character has some desire, something that they want. And then on the path on the journey of them to get that thing, they run into some kind of conflict. Okay. And what's interesting is that most of us, a big mistake we make in our stories, if we talk about the desire we have and we're trying to create emotion in our audience by like, Oh, I got this new car. I got it. And we're trying to create desire there, but desire is not, excuse me, but, um, emotion is not created by the desire. Emotion is created by the conflict is on the path to desire. Okay. It's the conflict that makes it interesting. Okay. So again, every character, uh, every hero's got a character, desire and conflict. And Michael, hey, when he, when he's teach about, he says the most simple form is you look like red riding red. Okay. Red ride, little red riding hood is a character. She had desires. She want to take cookies to her grandma. And the conflict is on the path to give her her grandma cookies. She ran into a big bad wolf. That's a story. That's it. Those are the three core things of a story. Okay. Now as we dig deeper into this, the next step is we have to have, we have to care about the character. Okay. The character, the hero, whatever you want to call it, we have to care about them. Um, have you guys ever watched a movie before where the whole movie happens and the character dies and you're like, yeah, well, I don't really like that much anyway. He has ever had that before. Come on. Okay. Come on. You guys have, um, if movies don't do a good job of the character development initially, then then it destroys the whole movie because nobody cares about the character at the end. So usually the first 10% of every single movie, the goal is to build a relationship with the hero. Okay. Now when Michael Hague, so there's five ways that, that in Hollywood, they try to build the relationships with the character and a good character usually has two of these five things. So first thing the character needs to be in two of these five things. They can be a victim. Okay. They could be in jeopardy. They need to be really, really likable. They can be funny or they can be powerful. So think about any of the movies that you guys love, the characters in those movies, at least two of those five things are there. Okay. And the first 10% of the movie is all about helping you to fill those things so that when the character embarks on their journey, you actually care. Okay. Now the other side is that each of these characters have a desire of what they want. Okay. Now the desires are usually one of four things when they want to win something, they're going on this journey, so they want to win a prize. Okay. Or they're going on this journey, so they need to retrieve something and bring it back or they are going on a journey to escape something bad or they're going somewhere to stop something. Okay. These are the four desires. They're typically in most every movie. One of those four things. Okay. So if you look at this, the, the, if you look at the path, right, the first 10% of, uh, of every movie is building the relationship with the character. And about the 10% mark in every single movie is where the character actually leaves on a journey. If you watch every movie, they don't, they, they start in a spot, but they physically, they don't just like, they're physically leaving where they're at and going on a journey. Okay. Frodo had to leave the Shire to go take the ring to Mordor. Right. Think about every movie they physically leave where they're at and they're going on this journey. Okay. Now typically there's a journey. We call it the journey of achievement. It's the top arc here in the picture. The journey of achievement. This is the journey that the character is aware of. Like I'm going over here to go get that thing or go do that thing. Okay. The journey of achievement. And this is the, this is the journey that all of the listeners and the, the viewers of the, of the show are very, very aware of is like, this is where we're going. That's the process. This is what we're trying to achieve. Right. The journey of achievement. Okay. Now the second journey is this one underneath this again, he calls it the heroes to journeys. The second journey is the journey of transformation. Now what's interesting is usually during the first 10% when you have the backstory with the character, you're learning about the character and you're learning about their identity, who they believe they are when they're leaving on this journey. Right. The things are important to him, things that hold true. So they go on this journey and they're trying to accomplish something, but in the process, if the story's right, they're not just going to accomplish something. They're going to transform as a person. Okay. If you look at the transformation, what happens is they're going from their old belief system to a new belief. And this is the actual journey that we all care about. This is the one that makes us, that makes us care about the movie long term. Okay. So if you look at this, um, at the bottom there, it's the transformation from, from their identity, who they think they are to who they're going to become. So that's the heroes to journeys. Now you could plug this into any movie, but I'm going to share one of my favorite movies with you because it's one that I loved watching my kids. How many of you guys have seen the movie cars? All right. So I'm going to walk you through how cars fits into this cause that's a fun one. And it works with most of them. Okay. All right. So if you look at this from a lightning McQueen, right? So lightning McQueen is the character, the very beginning of the movie, we fight, you know, the movie starts and he's on this racetrack and we know that he's got this desire to win the piston cup. And so he's going on this race, everything's happening. And you learned a lot about him, right? You learned that he's powerful. You learned that he's funny. This is like chow and like making, you know, so he's powerful. He's funny and everyone likes him. Right. Everything's happening. And then at the end of it, the opening scene, they tie and they're like, Hey, you don't win the piston cup, but nobody did. So we're going to go on this journey where you got to do another race. And if you win that, you're going to be part of it. Now, before lightning McQueen goes on this journey, something really interesting happens. Okay. It's after he said bye to everybody and you see a couple of things. You see that he's, he's, he works for, uh, or his sponsors, Rusty's, he's kind of embarrassed, like, ah, it's Rusty's. And it's like, it's kind of embarrassing. I want to be with, with, uh, with these guys, like they're way cooler. And like this kind of, uh, with Dynaco, cause Dynaco is like all the cool cars. You see this like insecurity has, which makes us bond with him as a character. And then he pulls into, uh, to Mac and he gets on the phone with his agent. He's like, Hey man. And the agent's like, Hey, I didn't see your race, but I heard it was great. And, uh, and you find out really quick that lightning McQueen doesn't have any friends. He, uh, his, uh, his agent asked me how many tickets you want for the big race. He's like, Oh, well, um, are you going to come there? He's like, I don't know if I'm going to be there, but he's like, okay, well, I got tickets for your friends. Let me know who they're going to be. He's like, okay. And you find out like he doesn't really have any friends. So instantly like we have this rapport with, with lightning McQueen because we see the powerful, we see the strong, we see all these different sides of him. And so we have, we care about him. Right. So then he jumps in Mac the truck and he goes on this journey physically, leaves the location and goes on this journey. Right. And so he's going in the back of the car on this path to, uh, to get what he wants and then what happens conflict arise, right? Mac, the car falls asleep. The back pops out. He wakes up almost in a wreck and he starts going crazy. Got lands and radiator springs and, uh, he gets caught. They put him in jail. They put a boot on him and all these people he meets and, uh, and he's trying to get this thing like he has this desire. Like I need to get here to win the piston cup, but I can't because I have to do these things like you have to rebuild the road. You have to do this. Yeah. All these things he has to do. Every time you try to escape, he can't, he's pulling back in, he's pulling back in and he's going, like he's the entire movie's trying to achieve this thing. And we keep hearing about this journey of achievement that he wants to get, right? Over and over and over again. And during this process that he's doing that, um, all these things are happening to him as a character that makes us interested. All these experiences, all these things happen. And finally, then the movie, he's able to leave radiator springs and he gets to, to California to go and race for the, for the piston cup. And he goes out there and starts racing. And at first he's racing and his heart's just not in his like, call like, I want to win this, but like, I miss all my friends. I just made all these things are happening. He's out there racing and racing. And, uh, towards the end of the scene, um, his, uh, uh, all his friends from radio springs come, uh, doc and everybody and they help him in the race. He's going through this race and he's getting down to the very, very end. And, uh, they've got like one lap left and he's winning and he's going and, uh, he realizes he's about to win the piston cup. Like the journey of achievement, he's about to get it. The entire movie, we're all so excited. He's going to get this thing. I even fallen on his whole journey for two hours. We're so excited for him to win this thing. And then what happens? Okay. Chick Hicks, who's been number two behind the king for all these years. It's like, well, I'm not going to lose to the king again. So he goes and he rams the king and the king, his car flips out of control. Boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. And this just, just destroyed. Lightning Queens running is like about to achieve this thing. I'm going to dream out my entire life. And also he looks up in the scoreboard and he sees the king's car smashed on the, on the sideways. And he's like, huh. And all of a sudden this is where we get to learn about who Lightning McQueen has become. What happens is he goes, he slams on his brakes, comes right to the finish line and stops and stops. And then Chick Hicks flies past, wins the race. Lightning McQueen backs up. He drives over to the king. He gets behind the king and he starts pushing him. And he pushes them all the way to the finish line. And as he's pushing him, the king says, rookie, what are you doing? He says, don't you understand? You just gave away the piston cup. So this is the journey you've been trying to achieve this your whole life. You just gave it away. And he says, you know what, uh, old race car once told me, it's all, this is an empty cup. There'll be other years. This one's about you. And he pushes them through the end. And what we see, what's so cool is even though Lightning McQueen never achieved what he was trying to do, the first journey never happened. The second journey of transformation did happen. That's why we love that movie. Okay. And so even though in your stories, the hero does not have to achieve the end result, half my story, I never actually finished the thing, right? Like it's the, it's the transformation that we care about. Like who did you actually become during this journey? That's what we care about. Okay. And so that's the heroes two journeys. That pretty cool, you guys. All right. Cool. Now what I want to do and I want to show you is how do we take that? Cause after Michael Hague taught us, I was like, oh, this is so cool. How do I do that with what I'm doing? Like, like that's, there's so many layers and complexity. I'm like, how do I simplify that? And so for years, like literally over four years, I was trying to figure some things out and, uh, and I want to show you guys what we come up with. And, uh, we call this inside the inner circle. Uh, we call this the Piftynibrid script. Basically inner circles, the ones we've been testing all these cool ideas on. And, and what, it's been amazing over the last six to eight months as we've been doing this, as I've been doing it inside of our business, a lot of you guys probably don't even know what's happening. And the results have been amazing. Has been watching inner circle members do it. Like the results have been just amazing company after company after company. So this is the script and this is how it works. Okay. Now it's very, very similar to the heroes to journey was broken in a way that we can tell stories very fast. Okay. So it starts over here with the backstory. This is the first part of where you tell a good stories, right? The backstory. We got to come back over here. Don't start over here in techno babel and come back here. Like what was the thing that got you in? You start telling this backstory. Now the goal, the backstory is to get rapport with your audience. So they actually care if you achieve the thing at the end. If they don't have rapport with you, they're not going to listen. Okay. There's reason why I tell stupid stories all the time because it builds rapport. Right. So they have the backstory. Now after you introduce the backstory and talk about the desire of where you want to go. Now there's two things we have to share with our audience. Number one are external desires. And these are the ones that most of you guys are comfortable with. Like, I wanted to lose weight. Oh, I want to make money. Oh, I want to do whatever. Right. And these are the ones that most, uh, most people feel comfortable sharing because it's like the surface level or whatever. Right. It's the heat. It's the journey of transformation or of achieving like, oh, yeah, I want that. I want to achieve that. And so you talk about the story. Like, yeah, I wanted to achieve this, but then you have to stop. And the second you have to share are your internal desires. Can the internal desires are the things that most of us don't typically ever share. We think them, we feel them, but we don't ever talk about it. And so as soon as you start sharing your internal desires, this is when people will relate to you because like, man, I can't believe you just said that. Like, that's how I feel. I don't ever talk about that with anybody, but I share that. And so to get to the internal desire, a lot of times you'll say, here's the external desire, I want to make a lot of money. And to get the internal, you usually ask why about seven or eight times. Well, why do you want to make a bunch of money? Well, I want to make a bunch of money because I never had money before. I think it'd be kind of cool. Okay. Well, why do you want that? Well, you know, um, I just got married and be cool. They'll support my wife. I want to get some day. Okay. Cool. Why do you want to do that? Well, you know, uh, my parents growing up were good supporters and, um, and I want to be like them, why is that? Why? And you keep getting deeper and deeper and deeper. And eventually you'll get to the real internal desires, which typically related to you want to feel loved, you want to feel acceptance, you want to, you want to change the world, you want whatever those things are that are internal to you. And so you share that like, I wanted to make money, all these things, but like, but I really wanted, I just wanted to help people. Man, I just wanted to get the respect of my wife or my kids or my dad or whatever that, that thing is. And when you share your internal desires, that's when people bond because that's what the same reason why your audience wants to go there as well, but they won't share it. They want to admit it. And when you're willing to, you become vulnerable like that. That's when people will relate to you. Okay. So talk about that. Then we talk, the next step here is, is the wall. You hit this wall on this journey. Now the wall, interestingly enough, if you look at this, the wall is the current opportunity that they're in. Okay. Before you went on this journey, you weren't an opportunity as well. Right. So think about that. Like what is the current opportunity that your audience is stuck in? What's the vehicle that they're stuck in? Cause that's the wall. Okay. And so you got stuck in the same wall they did probably, right? If, if your goal was to lose weight and you were stuck over here doing the Atkins diet, whatever you were stuck, like that was the wall you hit. And it's probably the same wall or a very similar wall to what your audience has experienced at the same time. So you hit this wall, this is the vehicle. I wanted that result, but I couldn't cause I was stuck here. Right. And so because of that, I had to go on a journey. Cause I could not accomplish what I wanted in this vehicle. I'm stuck. I can't get out. I can't get there. So you have to leave on a physical journey, leave the opportunity you're currently using to try to get that results. It's not working. And also you start on this journey and you start on this journey. And during this journey is where this happens. You have this epiphany, boom, and that epiphany leads you to this new opportunity. I found it, that vehicle wasn't for me. Like that wasn't the thing. I tried that. You're all tried. It just didn't work for me or for you, but I tried this and this is what happened. Okay. And so because of that, this is the new opportunity. I was so excited, but like, I was scared. Like, I don't know if it's going to work or not working. You start freaking out. It's okay. Well, based on this new opportunity, I think it's the way, what's the plan you created? Okay, I have a plan. I'm going to do this and I'm going to walk and this is what I'm going to do. This is how I'm going to get to that end result. And you create this plan. So the plan is taking you on this journey now. And if the plan ends like I had an idea, I was going to make a bunch of money. And I did like, there's no emotion in that. The emotion is in the conflict. So as you get this plan, you're going to execute, you start going on this path. And then somewhere you hit conflict. And what was it? Like what kept you from having the success you actually wanted? Like what was that conflict for you? Talk about the conflict. And after you get through that conflict, then you have two things. One is the, is the journey of achievement. You achieve that thing. Could pull the image back up on the slides. It's number one. Can we get the image back up here on the slides? Can number one, you experience achievement. You achieve that thing. Maybe you didn't achieve it. Like why even queen to achieve it? You talk about the achievement you got, but then more importantly, you talk about the transformation, the fulfillment you got. And that's where people connect with you. Cause that's what they want as well. They don't really care about the, the achievement. They care about the transformation. And when they see the transformation you had, that's what gets them to believe. That's what knocks the domino down. That's like, wow, that's the path he's been there. He understands where I've gone. And that's what gets them to want to follow you. Okay. Now I broke this down into some questions that are inside your book. So you can read through these. It'll make it easier, but it's basically there's eight questions. The backstory, your desires, the wall, the epiphany, the plan, the conflict, achievement and transformation. Okay. So the first thing is the backstory. So I had these questions cause like when I'm trying to tell these stories, a lot of times I'll go off on a retangents and I, you know, five minutes later, I find I'm clear over here and I've missed the whole story. Right. So I have one of these on my desk I look at so I can remember I stay within the, the boundaries of good story structure. So I'll say, so if you look at this as the backstory, what is your backstory that gives you, that gives us a vested interest in your journey. So you ask that question and then you tell all the people in your backstory about why they should care about you. Okay. Number two, what were the desires? What is it you were trying to accomplish? And then what were the external struggles you were afraid of? And then what was the internal? What was the real reasons? Okay. Then what was the wall? Boom. What was the wall? The problem that you hit within your current opportunity that would not allow you to get that thing you wanted. Okay. And then boom, what was the epiphany that you experienced? That led you to this new opportunity after the epiphany. And what was the plan you created to go and get that thing? So you create the plan. I just run going, you create the plan, you start going on this thing. And then as you're going forward, that thing, what's the conflict you hit? You get stuck in. You all had conflict, right? I can't get through this thing. And after you figure out the conflict and you succeed, what was the, um, what was the achievement you had? What was the end result you achieved? And then number nine there is what was the transformation that you experienced? That's, that's the path. All right. How many of you guys want to hear me do an epiphany bridge story and fall in that script? Yeah. You have to be more excited than that, or I'm just going to go home. Okay. All right. So me guys have heard this story before. It's interesting. I'm going to, before I tell a story, I was hoping, I was planning to do a whole session on, on this topic, but events always change and tweak. How many of you guys have had a chance to go through the perfect webinar process? Okay. So just those who've gone through, if you haven't go through it in this part of the, I'm going to make more sense. Just really quick on it. I want to show you guys a really cool thing. If you look at the perfect webinar, if you look right here, there's the, the one thing and then there's three secrets. If you look at how I do the webinar, it's basically outside of the stack in the clothes, it's me telling four epiphany bridge stories. My first story is my origin story. I always spell it when we're wrong. I hope I got that right. The first one is the origin story that I tell about the thing. And sometimes that's enough to get people to, to buy whatever it is you are. And then typically happens after you tell the origin story about why this vehicle is the thing that you chose, why I chose funnels, why I chose ketosis, why I chose whatever fill in the blank for you. That's the first story I tell. And normally that happens that some people will buy immediately, but someone like that's cool. And they're like, Oh, but what if, and also they start having these, these faults, beliefs come up. Okay. And belief number one is typically about, about the vehicle. Number two is about their internal beliefs. Number three is about their external beliefs. And all I'm doing to each one of these as well, is I'm telling the epiphany bridge story about why I believe this vehicle is the right vehicle. And then I tell them epiphany bridge story about why I believe that internally, I was able to do it and why they can do it. And over here, the external things. That's kind of going deeper. I could spend two hours on this part alone, but I just wanted to share that for those who were doing the webinar to understand that's how it fits in. There's a epiphany bridge story. That's your origin story. There's epiphany bridge story about the vehicle, the internal and the external. If you go through the perfect webinar, that'll make more sense. But that's the key. It's basically four stories. And I move into the stack and the, and the closes and it's, it's really powerful. All right. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to go through and tell you guys epiphany bridge story the best I can. All right. So it starts with a backstory. So I'm going to embarrass my wife. She's here. So I hope she's not going to kill me, but, um, so my backstory started, uh, this is 15 years. We're going to be married 15 years this summer, which is amazing. Is that cool? Yeah. And so our first year after we got married, uh, we, I had never had a job before. She was working to support me and her and everybody. Like it was, it was amazing. And, um, after we first got married, uh, we had a little bit of credit card debt. We wrapped up and we're like, we couldn't, we couldn't, um, move forward because we were stuck. And so we sat down and we're like, we're going to be successful. And like we want to have a home someday and, and all the things we want to have kids some days, like we had to figure out how to get out of debt. And so this is a picture we took in the very first duplex we were renting. Um, and it's a picture of her and me cutting up our credit cards and, uh, look at it. Look at how much of a dork I am. He managed to, she married me like it's pretty bad, huh? Anyway. And so we were sitting there and it was just like, it was really like we were joking, making fun about the whole crime thing, but it was an emotional thing. Like, God, like, I know we want, like we want to like have a family and kids and all these amazing things, but we can't because all these things are working, aren't, aren't working. So we have to stop like what we're doing. We have to cut up our credit cards and figure out a different way. Right. And so my desire was like, I got to figure out as a man, like, how do I support her? I'm wrestling in college. I didn't have the ability to get a job. I'm like, I got to, I have desires to help her. I want to make, I want to make money. That was my, my first goal. But if you want to be completely honest, it wasn't just I wanted to make money. Um, I knew that after we first got married, I was going to wrestling practice and having fun. I was going to school and having fun. I come home at night and she'd worked an entire day during the day. And at night she would go and she'd serve table tonight through to have the money so we could pay our rent and so we could eat. And honestly, as like a new husband to know that your wife working two jobs while you're having fun, like living your dreams, it's hard. And I felt guilty and I felt bad. I was like, like, I want to, I want to be part. I want to help in this relationship. Right. And so I had some desires. Like I got to figure this thing out and I couldn't get a job. Cause I wanted to, I needed, I wanted to wrestle. Like that was my thing. I couldn't quit wrestling. So I had to figure out some other way to make money. And so I started this journey to go out there and initially I was starting making this little website. So me, I've heard this story before. Um, we live in Boise, Idaho. And, uh, if you've been to Boise, there's like potatoes on the ground everywhere. That's not actually true, but a lot of people think it is. Um, but, uh, one spring break, one of my buddies, this is Nate Plone. Uh, he was on the wrestling team with me and we were two, the only two married guys on the wrestling team. So for spring break, all of our buddies were down in Wendover and wherever, gambling and having fun. And we were, had our wives working and we're the unemployed husbands who felt guilty. So we didn't go on party. We sat at home and we're like, what should we do all day? He's like, I don't know. I want to make a potato gun. I'm like, I don't know that means, but I'm in, let's do it. So we spent the whole week, I make a potato guns, had so much fun shooting potatoes that weekend. And, um, I have a longer version of the story I can tell, but I don't have time. So anyway, we're shooting potato guns, having the time of our lives. And then went back to school next week. And then I remember, um, uh, after that, I was, I'd been learning about internet marketing at the time. And, uh, and I talked to, to Nate. I was like, Hey, I think I want to try to sell something online. And I want to, I want to sell potato gun plans. And I did some research and I looked at the time there were 18,000, 18,000 searches a month for the keyword potato gun plans and nobody was selling out. So I'm like, cool, I can sell potato gun plans. I'm going to be the potato clan guy. So I talked to Nate, I convinced him to go and film a DVD with me. So we went out and we filmed the DVD last year at funnel hacking lab. I gave everybody, how many of you has watched the DVD after you went home? Like two of you guys. It was so cool. I was such a nerd. Anyway, so we built this thing and it was awesome. We were having some fun with it and we started selling it. And then we hit this wall. Okay. Something really, really bad happened. Google slapped me. I was selling it on Google. I was, uh, I think at the time we were spending about 10 to $15 a day and we're making one or two sales a day. So we're making 30 to 50 bucks a day. And it was like the greatest thing in the world. And all of a sudden Google slapped me and it was like, it was done. I couldn't, I couldn't make any money anymore. And I was stuck. I was stuck in this vehicle. I'm like, well, I guess that was the dot com bust. I missed the bubble, right? And I'm like, oh, I gotta start paying attention to school again. Dang it. I was so mad, right? And then, uh, I had this epiphany. In fact, it wasn't epiphany. One of my friends called me. He was like Russell. And at the time he had a similar website, a business to mine. He had a bunch of these little websites and he was selling them. He said, look, all my websites stop working because Google changed the algorithm and prices went up. He's like, by started doing this thing, I started adding upsells to my pages. He said that when I found about 20, 30% of people bought the first product started buying the upsell and completely changed my business. I'm back on Google. We're making money again. I was like, are you serious? So I went back to my old potato gun DVD. I found a guy in Northern Idaho who actually made potato guns and did a deal with him when he dropped ship potato guns to people who bought my plans. Except an upsell had my very first upsell in place and we started making money. And I was like, I'm back. Like we're back in business. And I was so excited that my little potato gun business has now I spend in $20 or $30 a day and I was making like $100 a day. And I was like, I'm the richest kid on campus right now. This is amazing, right? It was so cool. And so because of that, I devised this plan. I was like, okay, I didn't potato market. It's like, where else can we do it? And so my slightly evil plan was to do this a million times. And so we did it in the weight loss market. We did it in the speed reading market. We did it in baby sign language. We did it in apps. We did it with couponing, which by the way is one of the hardest markets ever. Don't sell things that people are couponners. They are naturally, they naturally do not want to buy things. We did a seminar, a couponing seminar, and we handed out $1 certificates for ranch dressing and there were two 75 year old women in the front seat fighting over coupons. I was like, they're not going to give us anybody. They're fighting over dollar coupons. Anyway, horrible market. We actually figured out a way to make those guys buy, which was insanely cool. We tried it network market, tried all sorts of places. It was amazing. We were launching funnel after funnel after funnel after funnel. In fact, we launched 12 new companies one year. I thought it was so smart, but it was horrible. And so that's when I hit this conflict. I realized, look, for me to keep launching these little funnels, like I have to have a designer, a programmer, a copywriter, uh, and then be taking like eight to 10 people. Like that was the problem for me to get one little funnel live. I'd have the entire staff of people. It was costing me anywhere from, you know, 20 to $50,000 a month as it kept growing and it got harder and harder and harder. We couldn't do it anymore. And luckily that time I had this friend named Todd Dickerson and he flew to Boise and we were hanging out one day in front of whiteboard and he said, what if we create something so I could quit making you a new funnel every single week? I was like, is it really that bad? He's like, yeah, we do the same funnel every week. I don't want to do it anymore. What if we made this thing and we could make a website builder, we could just like have the templates and put things in place. I was like, that sounds awesome. And so we started building this thing that became click funnels. And that's kind of Dylan. He came out, he had this editor, he had this thing. We started putting those things together. And soon we had this amazing program where I could actually build funnels by myself. I didn't need to tech team of 50 or a hundred people. And the coolest thing in the world happened. I was able to achieve my dreams. I was able to make a business, make money. My wife was able to not have to work. We had a bunch of kids with a cool thing for me. Sit picture my kids. I kind of hang out with my kids now. My kids are building funnels with us. How cool is that? I get some most amazing experience in the world. And it's completely transformed my life. And that is the Piffing Bridge script.
